text.title: AI Code Review Checklist Core Rules
text.summary: Twelve checks every AI-generated diff must pass before merge to a protected branch. Each check carries a hard verdict (block / request-changes / approve-with-note). These are testable rules — reviewer judgment overrides only with written rationale in the PR.

rule.statement: Every import / require / use statement resolves to a package present in the lockfile (package-lock.json, poetry.lock, Cargo.lock, go.sum, pnpm-lock.yaml). If the diff adds an import that is not in the lockfile, BLOCK until either (a) the package is added via the project's package manager or (b) the import is removed.

rationale.source: Snyk & Lasso Security 'slopsquatting' research (2024-2025); Bar-Lev et al. on LLM package hallucination

rule.rationale: LLMs invent package names that "sound right" (left-pad-strings, react-validate-form). Attackers register the hallucinated names on PyPI/npm pre-emptively. Lasso found ~20% of LLM-generated package suggestions point to non-existent packages; some hallucinated names are now squatted with malware.

example.title: Hallucinated npm package

rule.statement: If the diff deletes test files, removes test cases, or adds skip/xfail/it.skip/it.only/test.skip markers, BLOCK unless the PR description explicitly justifies each removal with a test-coverage diff. Skip markers added to make CI green are a hard block.

rationale.source: 2024 Microsoft internal study on Copilot test-modification behavior (cited in DevX whitepaper)

rule.rationale: When AI is asked to "make tests pass" without further constraint, the cheapest behavior is to skip or delete the failing test. Reviewers miss this because the PR title says "fix tests" and CI is green — exactly the signature of a silent regression.

rule.statement: Diff must conform to project conventions documented in repo root (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, CONVENTIONS.md, .editorconfig, lint config). Concretely: naming style for new symbols, file layout for new modules, error-handling pattern. If diff introduces a foreign idiom (e.g., callback style in a codebase that uses async/await everywhere), REQUEST_CHANGES with link to the convention doc.

rationale.source: GitHub Copilot 2024 productivity studies; Faion gap analysis pain #1

rule.rationale: AI agents follow the patterns most common in their training data, not the patterns in this repo. Convention drift compounds: once one AI-generated file uses callbacks, the next prompt sees both styles and the codebase fractures.

rule.statement: Run a secret scanner (gitleaks, detect-secrets, trufflehog) on the diff. Any positive hit → BLOCK. Also check for hardcoded test fixtures with real-looking PII (real emails, real phone numbers, real names) — these read as plausible test data to AI but are exfil risk if they're real user data the AI saw in context.

rationale.source: GitHub Copilot Secret Scanning data (2024); Anthropic Claude system prompt audits

rule.rationale: AI can paste secrets from context window into "example" code blocks. Test fixtures generated by AI sometimes contain real PII the AI saw in a different file in the same session.

rule.statement: Every new try/catch, error return, or Result-style branch is examined: does the handler do something useful (log + rethrow, return typed error, retry with backoff), or does it swallow the error? Swallowed errors (empty catch, catch-and-log-only on a write path, except: pass) → BLOCK.

rationale.source: Stanford 'Do Users Write More Insecure Code with AI Assistants?' (Perry et al., 2023)

rule.rationale: Empirical: AI-assisted developers wrote significantly more code with silent failure paths than the control group. The 'plausible-looking' catch block is one of the top AI smell-codes.

rule.statement: Any diff touching authentication, authorization, session handling, JWT/cookie code, RBAC checks, or permission decorators → REQUEST_CHANGES requiring a senior reviewer sign-off. AI is known to introduce subtle auth regressions (missing tenant_id filter, wrong scope check, broken CSRF token validation).

rationale.source: OWASP LLM Top 10 (2024); empirical agent-auth incident reports

rule.rationale: Auth code has the highest blast radius and the lowest tolerance for plausible-but-wrong. The pattern "AI fixed the failing auth test" frequently means the AI weakened the check to make the test pass.

rule.statement: Any new raw SQL, ORM mutation outside a transaction, or DELETE/UPDATE without explicit WHERE assertion → REQUEST_CHANGES requiring confirmation of (a) parameterization (no string concat), (b) transaction boundary, (c) row-count guard (delete that would affect >N rows must error, not proceed).

rationale.source: OWASP A03 Injection persistence; AI-generated SQL audit by Snyk

rule.rationale: AI confidently generates string-concatenated SQL when it has not been prompted with parameterized examples from the codebase. ORMs reduce but do not eliminate this; raw query escape hatches are the common vector.

rule.statement: Diff that pulls a new third-party dependency must include the dependency license in the PR description and check it against the project's allowed-license list. Diff containing large prose blocks (READMEs, license texts, large config snippets) that the AI may have regurgitated verbatim from training data → search for distinctive 8-word phrases on the public web to rule out copy-paste from a copyleft codebase.

rationale.source: GitHub Copilot 'code suggestions matching public code' setting; AGPL contamination case law (2024 Bunkr v. open-source)

rule.rationale: AI assistants can emit large blocks of memorized code that match GPL or AGPL sources verbatim. Importing such a block silently re-licenses the project.

rule.statement: Loops over potentially-large collections, N+1 query patterns, unbounded recursion, sync calls on hot paths, and missing pagination on list endpoints → REQUEST_CHANGES. AI loves the readable-but-O(n²) solution.

rationale.source: LLM-generated code complexity audits (Liu et al., 2024); Discord engineering postmortems on Copilot-introduced N+1s

rule.rationale: AI minimizes for code clarity, not big-O. The default suggestion for "filter users with active subscriptions" is a nested loop, not a JOIN.

rule.statement: Diff that adds new functions, classes, or files with no callers and no test coverage → REQUEST_CHANGES. Either remove the orphan or add the integration point that uses it. AI frequently generates helpers it never wires up.

rationale.source: AI agent task-completion failure mode (Anthropic Claude tool-use eval, 2025)

rule.rationale: When an agent gets distracted mid-task, it leaves half-built scaffolding. The half-built helper compiles, looks reasonable, and ships dead weight that future readers assume is load-bearing.

rule.statement: If the diff title says "fix" but the change is actually a workaround (added a sleep, added a TODO comment, added a feature flag to disable the failing path, broadened an except clause), label as DEBT not FIX and re-route through the tech-debt-management methodology. Do not merge as a fix.
rule.rationale: "AI fixed it" PRs that ship workarounds are the dominant source of architectural decay in AI-heavy codebases. Labeling honestly preserves the ability to find and pay the debt later.

rule.statement: PR description must declare which AI tool generated which portion (Copilot/Claude/Cursor/agent name) and approximate % of new lines that were AI-generated. This is for traceability, not blame. If the field is missing, REQUEST_CHANGES with a one-line attestation requirement.

rationale.source: EU AI Act provenance requirements (2025); regulated-industry audit practice

rule.rationale: Future debugging, security audits, and IP/license investigations need to know which lines came from which generator. Capturing the metadata at PR time is 1000× cheaper than reconstructing it later.
